title: Monotonic stack and queue

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Monotonic stack and queue; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Monotonic stack and queue
sources.claim: A monotonic stack or deque removes candidates that cannot be the next greater, smaller, minimum, or maximum answer.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A monotonic stack or deque removes candidates that cannot be the next greater, smaller, minimum, or maximum answer.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A next-greater stack pops smaller values when a new greater value resolves them. Your goal is to implement and explain a next-greater or range-extreme problem with a monotonic structure. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Arrays, strings and matrices; Hash tables, sets and maps; Stacks, queues and deques; Stack ADT; Deque ADT; Correctness and counterexamples. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A monotonic stack or deque removes candidates that cannot be the next greater, smaller, minimum, or maximum answer. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: Across C, C++, Java, and Python, keep the state transition and invariant language-neutral first; then map indexes, numeric overflow, recursion, mutable collections, hashing, and library operations to the selected language and retest their guarantees.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Strict versus nonstrict comparison controls duplicates, and stored indexes are often needed for distance and expiry. The draft therefore rejects: A monotonic stack must contain every previously visited element.

includes: Discard dominated candidates while preserving ordered potential answers.
Implement and explain a next-greater or range-extreme problem with a monotonic structure.

Across C, C++, Java, and Python, keep the state transition and invariant language-neutral first; then map indexes, numeric overflow, recursion, mutable collections, hashing, and library operations to the selected language and retest their guarantees.
excludes: Strict versus nonstrict comparison controls duplicates, and stored indexes are often needed for distance and expiry.

outcomes: Implement and explain a next-greater or range-extreme problem with a monotonic structure.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A monotonic stack must contain every previously visited element.
misconceptions.correction: Remove dominated candidates while preserving the monotonic invariant; discarded items cannot answer any future relevant query.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: A monotonic stack must contain every previously visited element.

limitations: A physical arrangement does not reproduce asymptotic cost, aliasing, numeric limits, recursion depth, or the four languages' implementation rules.
